payload.title: Endee Vector Database Documentation - Endee Docs

elements.content: Endee is a high-performance vector database designed for speed and efficiency. It enables rapid Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) searches for applications requiring robust vector search capabilities with advanced filtering and metadata support.
Fast ANN Searches: Efficient similarity searches on vector data using HNSW algorithm
Multiple Distance Metrics: Support for cosine, L2, and inner product distance metrics
Metadata Support: Attach and search with metadata and filters
Advanced Filtering: Powerful query filtering with operators like $eq, $in, and $range
High Performance: Optimized for speed and efficiency
Scalable: Handle millions of vectors with ease
Multiple Optimizations: 5 different quantization precision levels (BINARY, INT8, INT16, FLOAT16, FLOAT32) for speed and accuracy trade-offs
Hybrid Search: Combine dense vector search with sparse keyword search
Semantic Search: Build intelligent search systems that understand meaning, not just keywords
Recommendation Systems: Power personalized recommendations based on similarity matching
RAG Applications: Enhance LLM applications with relevant context retrieval for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
Image and Video Search: Find similar images and videos using visual embeddings
Here’s how to use Endee Vector Database in your applications:
Use an external embedding model to convert your data into dense vectors. Popular options include OpenAI embeddings, Sentence Transformers, or any embedding model of your choice.
Create an index using our SDK to store and organize your vectors efficiently.
Upsert vectors with unique IDs, metadata, and filters. Each vector can have associated metadata for filtering during search.
Use the same embedding model to convert your query text into a vector, then search the index to find the most similar results. Apply filters for more precise searches.
Ready to start building? Follow our guides:
Quick Start — Get running in minutes
Core Concepts — Learn how Endee works under the hood

payload.title: About Us - Endee Labs

elements.content: A team of relentless builders, pushing the boundaries of what's possible in AI infrastructure. We don't just store vectors, we make them fly.
When we started building AI applications, we hit a wall. Existing vector databases couldn't keep up with production demands. They were either too slow, too expensive, or couldn't scale.

So we built Endee from the ground up - a vector database engineered for speed, scale, and efficiency. Not just another storage system, but true AI infrastructure designed for the demands of production workloads.
Our Vector Graph Engine (VGE) represents years of research into graph algorithms, memory optimization, and hardware acceleration to deliver unmatched performance at scale.

But performance alone isn't enough. That's why we pioneered Queryable Encryption - a breakthrough that enables you to search encrypted data without ever exposing it. Your vectors remain encrypted end-to-end, giving enterprises true zero-knowledge security without sacrificing speed.
The visionary leading Endee's engineering innovation

Vineet is a seasoned tech entrepreneur with over 20 years of experience building and scaling high-performance technology platforms. His career spans distributed systems, AI platforms, and cloud-scale data infrastructure.

As the Founder of Endee, Vineet brings decades of deep technical expertise to building a next-generation vector database - engineered for relentless performance, effortless scalability, and real-world AI workloads. He is deeply passionate about algorithms, systems design, and the science of scale.

payload.title: Endee Vector Database Documentation - Endee Docs

elements.content: Endee is a high-performance vector database designed for speed and efficiency. It enables rapid Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) searches for applications requiring robust vector search capabilities with advanced filtering and metadata support.
Fast ANN Searches: Efficient similarity searches on vector data using HNSW algorithm
Multiple Distance Metrics: Support for cosine, L2, and inner product distance metrics
Metadata Support: Attach and search with metadata and filters
Advanced Filtering: Powerful query filtering with operators like $eq, $in, and $range
High Performance: Optimized for speed and efficiency
Scalable: Handle millions of vectors with ease
Multiple Optimizations: 5 different quantization precision levels (BINARY, INT8, INT16, FLOAT16, FLOAT32) for speed and accuracy trade-offs
Hybrid Search: Combine dense vector search with sparse keyword search
Semantic Search: Build intelligent search systems that understand meaning, not just keywords
Recommendation Systems: Power personalized recommendations based on similarity matching
RAG Applications: Enhance LLM applications with relevant context retrieval for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
Image and Video Search: Find similar images and videos using visual embeddings
Here’s how to use Endee Vector Database in your applications:
Use an external embedding model to convert your data into dense vectors. Popular options include OpenAI embeddings, Sentence Transformers, or any embedding model of your choice.
Create an index using our SDK to store and organize your vectors efficiently.
Upsert vectors with unique IDs, metadata, and filters. Each vector can have associated metadata for filtering during search.
Use the same embedding model to convert your query text into a vector, then search the index to find the most similar results. Apply filters for more precise searches.

payload.title: Concepts - Endee Docs

elements.content: Understanding the core concepts of Endee Vector Database.
Indexes are the vector stores where your data lives. Endee supports two types of indexes:
Dense Indexes: For semantic similarity search using vector embeddings
Hybrid Indexes: Combine dense vectors with sparse vector search
Both index types are optimized for fast Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) searches using the HNSW algorithm.
Dense indexes enable semantic search — finding items based on meaning rather than exact keyword matches. Use dense indexes when you want to:
Hybrid indexes combine dense vector search with sparse vector search (e.g., BM25 keyword matching). To create a hybrid index, specify the sparse model parameter along with the dense dimension.
Use hybrid indexes for document retrieval in RAG pipelines where you need both semantic and keyword matching.
Endee provides the endee-model Python package for generating BM25 sparse vectors. BM25 (Best Matching 25) is a proven keyword-based ranking algorithm that has powered search engines for decades.
The endee-model package provides two methods because BM25 weights documents and queries differently:
Queries are typically short (5-10 words), so applying length normalization would unfairly penalize them.
Endee BM25 should only be used with Endee Vector Databse. See the Endee BM25 guide for installation, code examples, and complete workflows.
When creating an index, you can configure the following parameters:
Choose the appropriate distance metric based on your use case:
The precision parameter controls how vectors are stored:
For hybrid indexes, include sparse query components:
Hybrid indexes support three query modes:
Dense only: Provide only the dense query vector for semantic search
Sparse only: Provide only sparse indices and values for keyword-based search
Hybrid: Provide both dense and sparse components for combined results
Query results include the following fields:
Vectors are the fundamental data units stored in an index.
For hybrid indexes, include sparse vector components alongside dense vectors:
Sparse indices and sparse values must have the same length.
Maximum batch size is 1000 vectors per upsert call.
Use filters to restrict search results based on conditions. All filters are combined with logical AND.
Operators are case-sensitive in SDK implementations
The range operator supports values within [0 – 999] — normalize larger values before upserting
When using filtered queries, two optional parameters let you tune the trade-off between search speed and recall.

Controls when the search strategy switches from HNSW filtered search to brute-force prefiltering. When very few vectors match your filter, HNSW may struggle to find enough valid candidates through graph traversal — in that case, scanning the filtered subset directly (prefiltering) is faster and more accurate.
Raising the threshold means prefiltering kicks in more often; lowering it favors HNSW graph search.

When using HNSW filtered search, some candidates explored during graph traversal are discarded by the filter, which can leave you with fewer results than requested. This parameter expands the internal candidate pool before filtering is applied to compensate.
Start with the defaults. If filtered queries return fewer results than expected, increase the boost percentage. If filtered queries are slow on selective filters, lower the cardinality threshold.
You can update the filters of an existing vectors by providing their IDs and a new filter objects. The update replaces the entire filter object — it does not merge with existing filters.
Filter updates are destructive replacements. Any filter keys not included in the new filter object will be removed from the vector.
By default, Endee runs without authentication for easy local development. To secure your instance, set the NDD_AUTH_TOKEN environment variable when starting the server.
When authentication is enabled, you must provide the same token in the following places:
Enter the token in the dashboard login to access the web interface at http://localhost:8080.
Pass the token when initializing the client. See the respective SDK’s documentation for details.
Include the token in the Authorization header:
All API requests will fail if the token doesn’t match the server’s NDD_AUTH_TOKEN. Keep your token secure and never expose it in client-side code.

When using filtered queries, two optional parameters let you tune the trade-off between search speed and recall.
Controls when the search strategy switches from HNSW filtered search (fast, graph-based) to brute-force prefiltering (exhaustive scan on the matched subset).

When very few vectors match your filter, HNSW may struggle to find enough valid candidates through graph traversal. In that case, scanning the filtered subset directly (prefiltering) is faster and more accurate. Raising the threshold means prefiltering kicks in more often; lowering it favors HNSW graph search.

When using HNSW filtered search, some candidates explored during graph traversal are discarded by the filter, which can leave you with fewer results than top_k. filter_boost_percentage compensates by expanding the internal candidate pool before filtering is applied.

payload.title: Endee BM25 - Endee Docs

elements.content: The endee-model package provides Endee’s BM25 model for generating sparse vector embeddings. These sparse vectors capture keyword/term importance and can be combined with dense embeddings for powerful hybrid search.
BM25 is asymmetric — documents and queries are weighted differently.
Documents benefit from TF weighting — if a term appears multiple times, it’s likely important
Documents are length-normalized — a long document shouldn’t score higher just because it has more words
Queries use IDF-only weighting — each query term gets equal importance
Rule of thumb: Use .embed() on text you’re storing. Use .query_embed() on text you’re searching with.
The model returns sparse embedding objects with indices and values arrays:
Here’s a complete example combining endee-model with the Endee client for hybrid search:
For large datasets, process embeddings in batches and save to JSONL format:
BM25 (Best Matching 25) scores documents based on:
Term Frequency (TF) — How often a term appears in the document (with diminishing returns)
Inverse Document Frequency (IDF) — How rare the term is across all documents (rare = more informative)
Length Normalization — Prevents long documents from having an unfair advantage

content: Endee Enterprise is a production-grade vector database designed for enterprise use, offering managed infrastructure, automated backups, seamless upgrades, and advanced security features. It enables organizations to focus on building AI applications without the overhead of database management.

Key features include:
- **Seamless Version Upgrades:** Zero-downtime updates with automatic rollback to avoid production disruptions.
- **Continuous Backups:** Automated, point-in-time recovery and continuous replication across availability zones to prevent data loss.
- **Fully Managed Infrastructure:** Endee handles provisioning, scaling, monitoring, and maintenance.
- **Enterprise-Grade Security:** Compliance with ISO 27001, SOC 2, and GDPR; includes role-based access control, audit logging, and end-to-end encryption.
- **Queryable Encryption:** Enables vector similarity searches on encrypted data without decryption, ensuring zero-knowledge security.
- **24/7 Dedicated Support:** Priority support with SLAs, a dedicated account manager, and direct engineering access.

**Queryable Encryption** is emphasized as a core capability, allowing encrypted data searches without exposing raw vectors or queries. Data is encrypted client-side, and compliance with major security standards is maintained.

The site provides a comparison between Endee and Endee Enterprise editions, an FAQ section, and invites enterprises to contact them for tailored solutions. Endee is positioned as high-performance vector infrastructure built for scalable, production AI systems.

page: https://endee.io/benchmarks
content: Endee is a high-performance vector database independently benchmarked using VectorDBBench. On the Cohere 10M dataset (768 dimensions), Endee achieved 1169 queries per second, 98.5% recall accuracy, and 9.6ms P99 latency, at a cost of $84 per billion queries. These results were compared against Pinecone, Milvus, Qdrant, and Zilliz Cloud.

Endee offers the highest throughput and lowest cost among tested databases, making it well-suited for production AI workloads. It maintains high recall and low latency, balancing accuracy and speed, while operating with just a tenth of the memory footprint of standard vector databases. This leads to significant reductions in infrastructure costs. All benchmarks are sourced from VectorDBBench.

page: https://docs.endee.io
content: Endee Vector Database Documentation

Endee is a high-performance vector database designed for rapid Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) searches, supporting advanced filtering and metadata. It is optimized for speed and efficiency, making it suitable for applications requiring robust vector search capabilities.

Key Features:
- Fast ANN searches using the HNSW algorithm.
- Supports multiple distance metrics: cosine, L2, and inner product.
- Metadata can be attached to vectors and used in searches.
- Advanced filtering with operators such as $eq, $in, and $range.
- Scalable to millions of vectors.
- Offers five quantization precision levels (BINARY, INT8, INT16, FLOAT16, FLOAT32) for balancing speed and accuracy.
- Hybrid search combines dense vector and sparse keyword search.

Use Cases:
- Semantic search for understanding meaning beyond keywords.
- Recommendation systems based on similarity matching.
- Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) for LLM applications.
- Image and video search using visual embeddings.

Workflow:
1. Generate Vectors: Use external embedding models (e.g., OpenAI embeddings, Sentence Transformers) to convert data into dense vectors.
2. Create Index: Use the SDK to create an index for efficient vector storage and organization.
3. Upsert Vectors: Add or update vectors with unique IDs and metadata for filtering.
4. Search with a Vector: Convert queries into vectors using the same embedding model and search the index, applying filters as needed.

Getting Started:
- Quick Start: Instructions to get running in minutes.
- Core Concepts: Guides explaining how Endee works internally.

content: Endee Labs Privacy Policy (Last updated: 3/24/2026)

Introduction  
Endee Labs Pvt Ltd (“Endee”, “Company”, “we”, “our”, “us”) operates a high-performance vector database platform and related services. This Privacy Policy explains how your information is collected, used, disclosed, and safeguarded when you use our website, vector database services, API, or interact with our platform.

1. Information We Collect

Account Information:  
When you create an account or request a demo, we collect your name, email, phone number, company name, job title, business information, billing/payment details, authentication credentials, and API keys.

Usage Data:  
We automatically collect API requests, queries, database operations, performance metrics (QPS, latency, recall rates), system/error/debug logs, IP addresses, browser/device info, and usage statistics.

Vector Data and Embeddings:  
We process and store vector embeddings and related metadata you upload. We do not access the semantic content of your vectors but retain technical metadata for operations, indexing, and optimization.

2. How We Use Your Information

Service Provision:  
We use your data to operate, maintain, and improve our platform, process queries, provide support, monitor/optimize performance, and manage accounts/billing.

Platform Enhancement:  
We analyze usage to improve performance, develop new features, research vector search algorithms, and optimize indexing/caching.

Security and Compliance:  
We detect/prevent fraud and threats, ensure compliance with our Terms of Use, protect service integrity, and meet legal/regulatory obligations.

3. Data Storage and Processing

Data Location:  
Your data is stored in secure data centers, with location depending on your plan/configuration. Safeguards are in place for international transfers per data protection laws.

Data Isolation:  
Each customer’s database is isolated with strict access controls, data segregation, and encryption at rest and in transit.

4. Information Sharing and Disclosure

We do not sell, rent, or trade your personal or vector data. Information may be shared only:
- With service providers (hosting, payment, analytics) under contractual data protection obligations.
- To comply with legal requirements or protect rights/property/safety.
- In business transfers (merger, acquisition, sale), subject to the same privacy protections.

5. Data Security

Security measures include:
- End-to-end encryption in transit (TLS 1.3)
- AES-256 encryption at rest
- Multi-factor authentication, API key management
- Regular security audits and penetration testing
- Access controls (least privilege), continuous monitoring
- Data backup and disaster recovery

No system is completely secure; you are responsible for keeping your API keys and credentials confidential.

6. Data Retention

- Vector Data: Retained during your subscription and 30 days after termination (unless you request earlier deletion).
- Account Information: Retained during your account and up to 7 years for billing/tax.
- Usage Logs: Retained up to 90 days for monitoring/debugging.
- Backup Data: Retained in encrypted backups for up to 30 days.

7. Your Privacy Rights

Depending on your jurisdiction, you may have rights to access, correct, delete, or restrict your data, request portability, object to processing, or withdraw consent. Contact contact@endee.io to exercise these rights; responses are provided within 30 days.

8. API and Integration Privacy

When integrating our API, you are responsible for:
- Having rights to upload vector embeddings
- Complying with privacy laws for processed data
- Implementing security in your applications
- Providing privacy notices to your end users

9. Cookies and Tracking Technologies

We use cookies for authentication, session management, analytics, preferences, and security. You can reject cookies in your browser, but this may limit platform functionality.

10. International Data Transfers

If you access services from outside India, your data may be transferred to/processed in India or other countries, with safeguards such as standard contractual clauses and compliance with data protection frameworks.

11. Children’s Privacy

The platform is for business/enterprise use and not intended for individuals under 18. We do not knowingly collect children’s data. Contact us if you believe such data has been collected.

12. Changes to This Privacy Policy

We may update this policy for changes in practices, technology, legal requirements, or business operations. Material changes will be notified via email or the platform. The “Last updated” date shows the latest revision. Continued use after changes means acceptance.

13. Contact

For questions or requests about this policy or data practices, contact:
Endee Labs Pvt Ltd  
Email: contact@endee.io

page: https://docs.endee.io/overview
content: Endee Vector Database is a high-performance system designed for rapid Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) searches, supporting applications that require robust vector search with advanced filtering and metadata capabilities.

Key Features:
- Fast ANN searches using the HNSW algorithm.
- Supports multiple distance metrics: cosine, L2, and inner product.
- Allows attaching and searching with metadata and filters.
- Advanced query filtering with operators such as $eq, $in, and $range.
- Optimized for speed and efficiency, scalable to millions of vectors.
- Offers five quantization precision levels (BINARY, INT8, INT16, FLOAT16, FLOAT32) for balancing speed and accuracy.
- Hybrid search capability: combines dense vector search with sparse keyword search.

Use Cases:
- Semantic search for understanding meaning beyond keywords.
- Recommendation systems based on similarity matching.
- Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) for LLM applications.
- Image and video search using visual embeddings.

Workflow:
1. Generate Vectors: Use external embedding models (e.g., OpenAI embeddings, Sentence Transformers) to convert data into dense vectors.
2. Create Index: Use the SDK to create an index for efficient vector storage and organization.
3. Upsert Vectors: Add or update vectors with unique IDs, metadata, and filters for enhanced search capabilities.
4. Search with a Vector: Convert query text into a vector using the same embedding model and search the index for similar results, applying filters as needed.

Getting Started:
- Quick Start: Instructions to get up and running quickly.
- Core Concepts: Guides explaining how Endee works internally.

Getting Started

Option 1: Docker Compose (Recommended)

Option 2: Build from Source

- If using authentication (NDD_AUTH_TOKEN), use the same token for dashboard login and SDK clients.
- You can also build and run a Docker image from source.

Access the Dashboard
- Once running, access the dashboard at http://localhost:8080.
- The dashboard lets you create/manage indexes, view statistics, and monitor query performance.
- By default, authentication is not required for development.

Connect Your Application

Python SDK

- Create and query an index.
- If using a custom domain/port, configure the base URL.

TypeScript SDK
- Install the Node.js package.
- Create and query an index.
- Configure base URL if needed.

Java SDK
- Add the dependency to your pom.xml.
- Create and query an index.

Go SDK

Direct API Access

Managing the Service

Data Persistence

page: https://docs.endee.io/concepts
content: Endee Vector Database: Core Concepts

Indexes  
Indexes are vector stores for your data. Endee supports:
- Dense Indexes: For semantic similarity search using vector embeddings (e.g., finding similar documents, powering recommendations, image/video similarity).
- Hybrid Indexes: Combine dense vector search with sparse vector search (e.g., BM25 keyword matching). Specify both a sparse model and dense dimension to create one. Useful for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines needing both semantic and keyword matching.

Both index types use the HNSW algorithm for fast Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search.

Endee BM25  
Endee offers the endee-model Python package to generate BM25 sparse vectors. BM25 is a long-standing keyword-based ranking algorithm used in search engines.  
- BM25 treats documents and queries differently; queries are short and not length-normalized.
- Endee BM25 is only for use with Endee Vector Database. See the BM25 guide for setup and examples.

Index Parameters  
When creating an index, you can configure:
- Distance Metrics: Choose based on your use case.
- Precision Options: Control how vectors are stored.

Querying  
- Query Parameters: Configure as needed.
- Hybrid Query Parameters: For hybrid indexes, include sparse query components.
- Query Modes for hybrid indexes:
  - Dense only: Use only the dense query vector (semantic search).
  - Sparse only: Use only sparse indices/values (keyword search).
  - Hybrid: Use both dense and sparse components for combined results.

Result Fields  
Query results include specific fields (not detailed here).

Vectors  
Vectors are the core data units in an index.
- Dense Vector Fields: For dense indexes.
- Hybrid Vector Fields: For hybrid indexes, include both dense and sparse components. Sparse indices and values must be the same length. Max batch size: 1000 vectors per upsert.

Filtering  
Filters restrict search results based on conditions (combined with logical AND).
- Operators are case-sensitive in SDKs.
- Range operator supports values [0–999]; normalize larger values before upserting.

Filter Tuning  
- Prefilter Cardinality Threshold: Controls when to switch from HNSW filtered search to brute-force prefiltering. Default: 10,000. Range: 1,000–1,000,000. Raise to prefilter more often; lower to favor HNSW.
- Filter Boost Percentage: Expands candidate pool during HNSW search to avoid too few results. Default: 0. Max: 100 (doubles pool). Increase if too few results; decrease threshold if queries are slow.

Updating Filters  
Update filters by providing vector IDs and a new filter object. Updates replace the entire filter object; omitted keys are removed.

Authentication  
- By default, Endee runs without authentication for local development.
- To enable authentication, set the NDD_AUTH_TOKEN environment variable when starting the server.
- When enabled, provide the token for:
  1. Dashboard access (login at http://localhost:8080)
  2. SDK clients (pass token during initialization)
  3. REST API (include token in Authorization header)
- All API requests fail if the token does not match. Keep the token secure; do not expose it in client-side code.

content: Python SDK Usage – Endee

This guide explains how to use the Endee Python SDK for core vector database operations: upserting, querying, and deleting vectors.

Setting Up Your Domain
- The Endee client lets you set a custom domain URL and port (default: 8080).
- Use set_base_url() if not using the default port.

Upserting Vectors
- Use index.upsert() to add or update vectors in an index.
- Specify required vector object fields.

Querying the Index
- Use index.query() for similarity search with a query vector.
- Supports various query parameters.

Filtered Querying
- Use the filter parameter to restrict search results; filters are combined with logical AND.
- The $range operator supports values from 0 to 999; normalize larger values before upserting.

Filter Tuning
- Two parameters control search speed vs. recall:
  - prefilter_cardinality_threshold: Switches between HNSW filtered search (fast, graph-based) and brute-force prefiltering (exhaustive scan). Raise the threshold for more prefiltering; lower it for more HNSW.
  - filter_boost_percentage: Increases the candidate pool size in HNSW filtered search to compensate for candidates discarded by filters. 
    - 0: no boost (default)
    - 20: fetch 20% more candidates
    - Max 100: doubles the pool
- Recommended: Start with defaults (prefilter_cardinality_threshold=10,000; filter_boost_percentage=0). If you get too few results, increase filter_boost_percentage. If queries are slow on selective filters, lower prefilter_cardinality_threshold (valid range: 1,000–1,000,000).

Updating Filters
- Use index.update_filters() to update filters for vectors without changing their data or metadata.
- Filter updates are destructive: omitted filter keys are removed from the vector.

Hybrid Search
- Hybrid indexes combine dense and sparse vector search. Create by specifying sparse_model.
- For BM25 sparse vectors, use the endee-model package (see Endee BM25 guide).
- To upsert hybrid vectors, provide both dense and sparse representations (sparse_indices and sparse_values, which must be the same length).
- To query a hybrid index, provide both dense and sparse query vectors.

Deletion Methods
- Delete by ID or delete an entire index. Deletions are irreversible.

Additional Operations
- Get vector by ID.
- Describe index.

API Reference
- See documentation for Endee and Index classes.

content: TypeScript SDK Usage – Endee

This guide explains how to use the Endee TypeScript SDK for core vector database operations: upserting, querying, and deleting vectors.

Setting Up Your Domain
- Configure a custom domain URL and port (default: 8080) for the Endee client.
- Use setBaseUrl() for non-default ports.

Upserting Vectors
- Use index.upsert() to add or update vectors in an index.
- Vectors require specific fields (see VectorItem).

Querying the Index
- Use index.query() for similarity search with a query vector.
- Accepts parameters (see QueryOptions).
- Returns an array of QueryResult objects.

Filtered Querying
- Use the filter parameter to restrict results; filters are combined with logical AND.
- The $range operator supports values 0–999; normalize larger values before upserting.

Filter Tuning
- Two parameters adjust search speed vs. recall:
  - prefilterCardinalityThreshold: Switches between HNSW filtered search (fast, graph-based) and brute-force prefiltering (exhaustive scan). Higher threshold triggers prefiltering more often.
  - filterBoostPercentage: Expands the candidate pool in HNSW filtered search to compensate for candidates discarded by filters. 0 = no boost (default), 20 = 20% more candidates, max 100 = double the pool.
- Default values: prefilterCardinalityThreshold = 10,000; filterBoostPercentage = 0. Adjust if queries return too few results or are slow.

Updating Filters
- Use index.updateFilters() to update filters for vectors without changing vector data or metadata.
- Updates are destructive: omitted filter keys are removed.

Hybrid Search
- Hybrid indexes combine dense and sparse vector search; specify sparseDimension to create one.
- See the Endee BM25 guide for generating sparse vectors.
- Upserting Hybrid Vectors: Provide both dense and sparse representations (sparseIndices and sparseValues must match in length and be within [0, sparseDimension)).
- Querying Hybrid Index: Provide dense, sparse, or both types of vectors for flexible querying.

Deletion Methods
- Delete by ID
- Delete by Filter: Remove all vectors matching specific filters.
- Delete Index: All deletion operations are irreversible.

Additional Operations
- Get Vector by ID: Returns a VectorInfo object.
- Describe Index: Returns an IndexDescription object.

Precision Options
- Endee supports multiple quantization levels (Precision enum):
  - BINARY: For very large datasets prioritizing speed and storage.
  - INT8: Recommended for most cases; balances accuracy and performance.
  - INT16 (default): Better accuracy than INT8, less storage than FLOAT32.
  - FLOAT16: Good compromise for embeddings.
  - FLOAT32: Maximum precision, highest storage.
- See Types Reference for details.

TypeScript Types

API Reference

- Index Class

content: Endee TypeScript Types – Summary

The Endee TypeScript SDK offers detailed type definitions to ensure type safety and IntelliSense. The documentation covers all available types, interfaces, and enums.

Importing Types:
Types can be imported directly from the endee package.

Enums:
- Precision: Specifies quantization precision levels for vector storage and search.

Type Aliases:
- SpaceType: Defines the distance metric used for similarity calculations.

Interfaces:
- CreateIndexOptions: Options for creating a new index.
- VectorItem: Represents a vector to upsert into an index. For hybrid indexes, sparseIndices and sparseValues must be the same length, and sparseIndices values must fit within the model’s dimension.
- QueryOptions: Options for querying an index. For hybrid queries, you can provide only a vector (dense search), only sparseIndices/sparseValues (sparse search), or all three (hybrid search).
- QueryResult: Represents a single result from a query.
- IndexInfo: Detailed information about an index returned from the server.
- IndexDescription: Simplified description of an index from index.describe().
- UpdateFilterParams: Represents a single filter update for index.updateFilters(). The filter object is replaced entirely; keys not present in the new filter are removed.
- VectorInfo: Detailed information about a single vector from index.getVector().

This structure ensures developers have clear guidance on type usage within the Endee TypeScript SDK.

page: https://docs.endee.io/java-sdk/types
content: The Endee Java SDK offers type-safe classes with builder patterns for compile-time safety. Types, enums, builders, and result classes are imported from the io.endee.client package.

**Enums**
- **Precision**: Specifies quantization precision levels for vector storage and search.
- **SpaceType**: Defines the distance metric for similarity calculations.

**Builder Classes**
- **CreateIndexOptions**: Used to create a new index with required name and dimension parameters, following the builder pattern.
- **VectorItem**: Represents a vector for upsert operations, requiring id and vector parameters. For hybrid indexes, sparseIndices and sparseValues must be the same length, and sparseIndices must be within [0, sparseDimension).
- **QueryOptions**: Used for querying an index. Supports dense, sparse, or hybrid search by providing vector, sparseIndices/sparseValues, or all three.

**Update Classes**
- **UpdateFilterParams**: Parameters for updating filters via index.updateFilters(). The entire filter object is replaced, not merged; missing keys are removed from the vector.

**Result Classes**
- **QueryResult**: Represents a single query result. Getter methods include getId(), getSimilarity(), getDistance(), getMeta(), getFilter(), getNorm(), and getVector().
- **IndexDescription**: Describes an index as returned by index.describe(). Getter methods include getName(), getSpaceType(), getDimension(), getSparseDimension(), isHybrid(), getCount(), getPrecision(), and getM().
- **VectorInfo**: Provides detailed information about a vector from index.getVector(). Getter methods include getId(), getVector(), getMeta(), getFilter(), and getNorm().

**Exception Classes**
- **EndeeException**: Base class for client-side errors such as network or serialization issues.
- **EndeeApiException**: For API-specific errors, extends EndeeException and includes HTTP status code and error body.

**Index Class**
- Returned by client.getIndex(name), this class provides methods for vector operations.

This structure ensures type safety and clarity when interacting with the Endee Vector Database using Java.

content: Go SDK Usage – Endee Vector Database

This guide explains how to use the Go SDK for core vector operations in Endee, including upserting, querying, and deleting data.

Setting Up Your Domain
- Configure the Endee client with a custom domain URL and port (default: 8080).
- Set BaseUrl if using a non-default port.

Upserting Vectors
- Use index.Upsert() to add or update vectors in an index.
- Each upsert can handle up to 1,000 vectors; vector dimensions must match the index, and IDs must be unique within the batch.
- VectorItem struct fields are used for vector data.

Querying the Index
- Use index.Query() for similarity search with a query vector.
- Results are returned as slices of QueryResult structs.

Filtered Querying
- Use the filter parameter to restrict query results; all filters are combined with logical AND.
- The $range operator supports values from 0 to 999; normalize larger values before upserting.

Updating Filters
- Use index.UpdateFilters() to update filters for vectors without changing their data or metadata.
- Filter updates are destructive: keys not included in the new filter map are removed.

Hybrid Search
- Hybrid indexes combine dense and sparse vector search; specify sparseDim to create one.
- The Go client does not have separate sparse fields—include sparse data with the dense vector in upserts.
- For queries, provide dense and/or sparse vectors:
  - Dense only: pass nil for sparseIndices and sparseValues.
  - Sparse only: pass nil for vector, provide sparse parameters.
  - Hybrid: provide all three for combined results.

Deletion Methods
- Delete by ID or by filter to remove vectors.
- For hybrid indexes, use dedicated hybrid deletion methods.
- Deletion operations are irreversible.

Additional Operations
- Retrieve vectors by ID.
- Get index information.

Context Support
- All operations support Go’s context.Context for cancellation and timeouts; use context-aware methods for request control.

Precision Options
- Endee supports various quantization precision levels via constants; see the Types Reference for details.

Error Handling
- The Go SDK returns standard Go errors, which should be handled using pattern matching.

API Reference
- Includes documentation for the Endee client and index operations.

page: https://docs.endee.io/go-sdk/types
content: The Endee Go SDK offers idiomatic Go types with exported struct fields and constants for interacting with the Endee Vector Database. All types and constants are accessed using the endee package prefix (e.g., endee.VectorItem).

Key components documented:

**Imports**
- Import the endee package to access all types and constants.

**Constants**
- **Precision Types:** Specify quantization precision for vector storage and search.
- **Distance Metrics:** Define the metric used for similarity calculations.
- **Limits:** Set maximum allowed values for various parameters.
- **Defaults:** Provide default parameter values when not specified.

**Structs**
- **VectorItem:** Represents a vector to be upserted into an index.
- **QueryResult:** Represents a single result from a query.
- **FilterParams:** Advanced filter parameters to balance search speed and recall during filtered queries.
- **FilterUpdateItem:** Represents a filter update operation for index.UpdateFilters(). Replaces the entire filter object; keys not present in the new filter are removed from the vector.

**Client and Index Types**
- **Endee (Client):** Main client type from EndeeClient(), used for managing indexes.
- **Index:** Returned by client.GetIndex(), provides methods for vector operations.

A type summary is also provided at the end of the documentation.

page: https://docs.endee.io/embedding-models/endee-bm25
content: Endee BM25 is a model provided by the endee-model package for generating sparse vector embeddings, which capture keyword or term importance. These sparse vectors can be combined with dense embeddings to enable hybrid search in the Endee Vector Database.

BM25 is an asymmetric model:  
- For documents, .embed() applies term frequency (TF) weighting and length normalization, so repeated terms and document length are considered.
- For queries, .query_embed() uses only inverse document frequency (IDF), giving each query term equal importance.

Best practice:  
- Use .embed() for text to be stored (documents).
- Use .query_embed() for search queries.

The model outputs sparse embedding objects containing indices and values arrays.

A typical workflow for hybrid search with Endee and BM25 involves:
1. Creating a hybrid index.
2. Generating embeddings and upserting them.
3. Querying with hybrid search.

For large datasets, embeddings can be processed in batches and saved in JSONL format.

BM25 scoring is based on:
- Term Frequency (TF): Frequency of a term in a document, with diminishing returns.
- Inverse Document Frequency (IDF): Rarity of a term across documents (rarer terms are more informative).
- Length Normalization: Prevents longer documents from scoring higher just due to length.

The documentation also provides API references for SparseModel and SparseEmbedding.
